17.1.label: Question 17.1
17.1.question:
State briefly how each of the following is likely to influence the claim amount for a PMI policy:
          hospital capacity
          medical science progress
          insurer / provider deals
          inflation.

Solution: Hospital capacity – Reduced capacity (ie reduced supply) will increase treatment costs, and hence
claim amounts will increase.
Medical science progress – This can lead to new treatments that may be expensive (increasing
costs), or be much more effective (reducing costs).
Insurer / provider deals – These are arrangements between insurers and providers (eg hospitals)
to provide specified treatments for policyholders at agreed costs. They will reduce the level of
cost of claims, reduce the claim cost uncertainty and hence reduce the variance of the claim costs.
Inflation – Costs of treatment will increase with inflation, so increasing claim costs.

17.2.label: Question 17.2
17.2.question:
Explain the principal concerns that the marketing manager and the valuation actuary will wish to
   consider when assessing the proposed design and pricing of a new health and care insurance
product.

Solution: The marketing manager will be primarily concerned with the attractiveness of the proposed
contract to the target market and to the salesforce, ...                                              [½]
... since it is often the case that the salesperson represents the main hurdle to the acceptability of
a health insurance product.                                                                        [½]
Free cover means obtaining cover free of underwriting. So applications up to the free cover limit
(eg a certain sum insured for a CI policy or a weekly benefit amount for an IP policy) will be
accepted, without any investigations beyond the completion of a basic proposal form or an
application to join a group scheme. The application may include a simple health declaration,
eg continuously at work for the last three months.
The RBNS and IBNR reserves represent future expected claims costs as at the end of the relevant
year. They should be included in the burning cost for that year because they relate to that year of
exposure (so they can correctly relate all claims to the appropriate exposure measure).
The previous year’s RBNS and IBNR must be deducted, because the claims to which they relate
will all be included again (only more accurately), in one of:
Thus the marketing manager\u2019s concer:           the perceived ‘value for money’ of the product in the client’s eyes                        [½]
          how well the product meets the needs of the target market                                  [½]
          any unique selling points – preferably at no extra cost                                    [½]
          the absence of explicit charges – such as a nil/reduced allocation period for unit‐linked
          attractive commission terms for salespeople and ease of selling                            [½]
The valuation actuary will be more conce:           the use of existing pricing and reserving methodologies                                    [½]
          not being overly risky, for example, the guarantees / options not being too onerous, and
          whether the extent of any cross‐subsidies leads to acceptable risk (eg from unexpected
changes in the mix of business)                                                    [½]
          setting sufficient premiums or charges to ensure adequate profitability and return on
          ensuring that profit is not too sensitive to changes in conditions, eg by making charges
          minimising new business strain (or ensuring there is enough capital to cover possible future
         a speedy return of initial capital                                                               [½]
         adequate underwriting standards and claims control                                               [½]
         ease of reporting and record keeping – including supervisory reporting                           [½]
         the effects on other business, both existing (eg lapses) and on sales of new business for
Syllabus objective: 4.2        Understand and apply the techniques used in pricing health and care insurance
0 Introduction: Most of the discussion so far on pricing healthcare insurance products has centred around
individual products. Whilst the same risk principles apply to the choice of assumptions for
group products, the market practices are somewhat different. These will be explored in this
The chapter will start by considering the characteristics that apply to group business that are
It then goes on to discuss the calculation of group business premium rates. These may be derived
as a weighted average of the insurer’s standard book premium rates (which are discussed in
Section 2) and the group’s own past experience. This method is a type of experience rating, and
Question: Suggest another reason why group insurance business book rates may be lower than individual
business premium rates.
Write down a possible formula for a group CI policy providing lump sum benefits to employees on
critical illness diagnosis only. Under the policy, all insured employees are entitled to critical illness
benefits equal to four times the rate of salary being earned at the time of diagnosis. Assume that
the policy is not experience rated.
Explain why the RBNS and IBNR reserves for the current year are added, and the previous year’s
values are deducted, in the burning cost calculation.
1.2 Control of intermediary: Recall that group business is usually sold through insurance intermediaries (brokers).
Quite often, the broker or intermediary will exert influence over the information available.
The scheme information may be channelled through the broker as might the revenues for
Actuaries will want to be sure that the information is as accurate as possible and that they
are getting the same level of detail as other companies who may also be pricing and
The level of detail provided on individual members is discussed in more detail in the next section.
There are further risk problems with the degree to which the insurer can influence the
employer’s attitude to risk and initial claims management, when all communication needs to
be passed through the broker. This, however, is not directly a pricing issue.
Brokers may be wary of threatening their relationships with clients by ‘imposing unpopular
measures’, such as requests for detailed information or carrying out regular risk assessments.
Though not directly a pricing issue, the actuary should reflect any factor that increases either the
level of, or the uncertainty around, future claims (the latter being important for the size of
margins to include in the basis).
1.3 Limited insured information: One of the risks involved in pricing group healthcare insurance products is that the detail of
individual lives insured (numbers, ages, gender split, benefits) is often not known by the
insurer at the time when the period of insurance begins.
The insurer, in such circumstances, will make an estimate of the premium to serve as a
deposit. This will be adjusted up or down at the end of the period, when the details are
These are often referred to as \u2018dep: Limited insured information for group schemes is becoming less of a problem more recently, with
full membership information usually being supplied at the quotation stage.
However, even where full details are known at the start of cover, retrospective adjustments will
need to be made to reflect staff changes over the period. Staff changes between periods of cover
will be considered in the next section.
1.4 Newcomers / leavers: The actuary will be particularly concerned about significant changes in the numbers and
profile of lives that comprise the group from one year to the next.
Change in profile: Of people leaving a particular employee group, it is arguably the fittest lives do so,
assuming them not to be the ill-health retirees, and thus the body of lives remaining are, on
This may be due to healthier people being more likely to change jobs in order to progress their
On the other hand, if most of the leavers are ill‐health retirees, then the opposite can be the case
– ie the remaining workforce is, on average, lower risk. This is a contributing factor to the
Reduction in group size: A large reduction in lives covered might indicate a down-sizing of workforce, which might
prompt more lives to claim for healthcare insurance while they are still covered.
Increase in group size: An increase in numbers within the group is generally a good feature of a scheme –
newcomers are often younger and fitter.
Care should be taken where a takeover has produced the influx or where there has been a
merger of schemes. The actuary must discover whether there is a claims history available
for this incoming group of lives; it might be significantly different from the experience of the
1.5 Flexible benefits: Some group products these days are designed to offer each employee a range of alternative
benefits, in place of mandatory life and healthcare covers. These could embrace many
aspects of the employee package and not just insurances.
As well as insurance benefits, the items on offer might include such things as buying more holiday
out of salary or making additional pension contributions.
From a pricing viewpoint, the actuary will need to be careful that the selection effect does
not permit advantage to be taken of the insurer, through the covers and amounts chosen.
Limits will generally be placed and statements of good health may also be requested.
It is the opportunity for individual choice – ie whether to take out insurance and for what levels of
cover – that leads to the anti‐selection effect.
2.1 Introduction: The calculation of group insurance business premium rates begins with the calculation of the
insurer’s standard risk premium (or ‘book rate’). This calculation is very similar to the calculation
Individual policy premiums are generally higher than group premiums, because the risk of
anti-selection against the insurer is higher when individuals are financing their own
This is particularly true where the group cover is compulsory for members.
Expenses for group business tend to be l:          lower levels of underwriting (as a result of lower levels of anti‐selection)
2.2 Risk cells: The book rates for a given portfolio should be consistent with individual product premiums,
though overall the group collectively may represent a better risk than the sum of the
individuals.
The comparison here is between the risk premium the insurer would charge to a private individual
taking out insurance and the rate that would be charged to the employer for providing identical
cover for employees having the same risk characteristics.
The Core Reading is saying that, while the rates may not be the same for the two cases – the
group‐insured rates may well be cheaper – they should be consistent. This means that the
relationships between the prices charged for the different risk groups (young/old etc) should be
similar for group and individual business.
The cost will be derived by splitting a relevant body of experience into appropriate risk
cells.
The data will be split according to factors such as age, gender and occupation.
For each risk cell, a historic risk cost or rate will be derived. This will then be adjusted to
be appropriate to the future body of lives and to the period of risk, ie adjusting for trends in
the potential to claim and for inflation.
These last steps are common to all assumption setting, as described in Chapter 13.
The overall premium that will be charged for a particular year of cover will be calculated as the
sum of the risk premium, expenses and a loading for profit. Group schemes are usually one‐ or
two‐year renewable, with the total premium being recalculated every (or every other) year.

2.3 Rating factors: Rating factors provide a means by which an insurance company can assess the correct book
premium to charge for any risk, or set of risks. These would be the factors, relating to the risk
presented by a specific policy, according to which the premium rate charged might be caused to
differ from that charged to others.
For group policies, the risk premium needs to reflect the overall risk characteristics of the group of
lives to be covered under the policy. Therefore there will be two different types of rating factors:
         individual‐level rating factors – these are rating factors that apply to the individual
members covered by the scheme and are therefore very similar to the rating factors that
would be used for individual business, for example, the individual‐level rating factors for a
CI policy might be age, gender, smoker s:          group‐level rating factors – these are rating factors that apply to the group itself, for
example industry and location, size of group, employer’s attitude to its employees’ health
Rating factors for group business are typically similar to those of individual business but
vary by product according to the relevance and amount of information provided. Generally
the larger the scheme, the more likely the price will depend on historical aggregate claims
experience and correspondingly less on individual-life rating factors.
So as well as individual‐level and group‐level rating factors, the insurer might use the group’s own
historical claims experience to calculate the premium for a group scheme, ie it might use
experience rating. Experience rating is discussed in the next section.
3.1 Experience rating: Experience rating is the phrase given to the practice whereby the premium for a group
contract depends wholly or partially on the past experience of that group.
The peculiarities of a specific workplace, the working processes and the employer’s attitude
to safety and healthcare can make a ‘book rate’ assessment of a company’s employee
healthcare insurance contract inappropriate. The particular risk features are possibly best
demonstrated by that group’s past claims history, suitably adjusted to make it relevant to
Essentially, the claims experience from a group contract is very much influenced by the particular
characteristics of the group, for example by the nature of the occupation(s) involved. Changes in
economic conditions can affect group claims experience differently for different employers,
depending on which sectors of the economy are most affected and also on how the particular
employer chooses (or is able) to deal with a particular situation.
Some form of experience rating is usually incorporated in PMI or IP group insurance
pricing, whereas individual business is normally priced on a community rate basis, ie the
premium is assessed with all other lives in the same risk cell without reference to individual
Note that there are two different meanin:          the practice of charging all policyholders (or a significant subset of policyholders) the
same premium rate irrespective of rating factors such as age, gender and medical history
– this is the meaning that was appropriate in Chapter 4 in the context of rating individual
         the process of applying tabular rates to applicants irrespective of claims history – this is
the meaning that is being used in the context above.
3.2 Credibility: The term credibility relates to the factor, between 0 and 1 inclusive, which represents the
proportion of the final risk premium that is derived from past experience, the balance
The value of the factor depends on the size of the scheme and a very large body of
experience would be needed to justify a factor of 1, ie the premium is 100% based on past
claims history. However, commercial pressures and market practices in many territories
have given full credibility to many schemes for group PMI, where less than 100 man-years of
information are available; group IP insurance demands somewhat more for full credibility.
So if E is the insurer’s standard risk premium (or book rate, as described in Section 2) for PMI
benefits for the group, and A is the equivalent risk premium based on the past experience of the
group, then the risk premium charged would be calculated as:
## RP \uf03d Z \uf0b4 A \uf02b \uf0281 \: where Z is the credibility factor for this group scheme  0  Z  1 .
As the Core Reading says, the value of Z will depend mostly on the size of the scheme and the
number of years of data available (and hence on the credibility of the past data upon which the
value of A is based). However, other factors may be involved if these affect the reliability with
which the future experience of a group scheme can be predicted from its past data, or if they
affect the relevance of the data to predicting future experience.
Sometimes an insurer will move away from its theoretical credibility values for competitive
reasons, and will in any case be likely to have a similar approach to its experience rating to the
other main insurers in the group market.
The terms of the experience‐rating structure will usually be specified in the conditions of the
group insurance scheme, including the method for calculating the experience risk premiums from
the data and the historical time period that will be used for the assessment. The time period
chosen becomes one year more recent for each year’s new premium calculation.
The credibility factor may also change in future years (ie the insurer will generally wish to keep
the factor under review). The factor could even be specified to be a function of experience
3.3 Burning cost: The burning cost is the accumulation of claims in one or more recent years, which might be
taken as a first measure of premium adequacy.
The following definition appears in the: ‘Typically the burning cost is the estimated cost of claims in the forthcoming insurance
period, calculated from previous years’ experience adjusted for changes in the numbers
insured, the nature of cover and medical inflation. The term can also be used to describe
the historic cost of claims only.’
So, in checking premium adequacy, the burning cost needs to be compared against the (risk)
premiums paid for the relevant period.
The burning cost would also need to include estimates of all claims reported but not settled,
and claims incurred but not reported. The concept has more application for group PMI and
group CI insurance than it has for group IP insurance where claims are paid out over
several years from a single incidence – though it can be used for IP insurance if an estimate
for claims in payment is included, ie if the expected future claims can be capitalised.
That is, for IP it would be necessary to include the expected present value of all future claim
payments arising from sickness inceptions occurring during the relevant year.
Burning cost calculations provide the earliest possible feedback on the adequacy of the insurance
Example: Ignoring adjustments for changes in exposure, inflation etc, estimate the burning cost for claims
occurring in the year 2019, based on the following information:

Reserve for claims incurred but not reported (IBNR)               200               150

         for CI: the total sum insured (ie to obtain a rate per £1,000 sum insured)
3.4 Relevance of previous experience: The pricing actuary will look at the past claims history available to judge the extent to which
it might be considered a good proxy to the future risk experience.
\uf0b7 in the cover required: are among the factors that can alter the applicability of past experience to the future.
The actuary will have to make suitable adjustments to the data before using it as a base for
These are considerations the actuary will normally make when deciding how to use any past
experience data for setting pricing assumptions. The difference with group contracts is how the
insurer can often look at the experience of the particular group policy itself to assess the pricing
4.1 Introduction: Pricing group PMI business sometimes differs slightly from the theoretical approach described in
the previous section. This section considers how and why this may be the case.
4.2 Experience rating: Some form of experience rating is usually incorporated in group PMI pricing at a level below
that which a strict statistical approach would demand.
As mentioned above, this may be due to commercial pressures and market practice.
The greater frequency of claim that applies with group PMI permits a wider usage of
experience rating where full or partial credibility will be afforded to past history, depending
on the number of member years that are available at the time of pricing.
Claims are more frequent on PMI compared to other types of health insurance, such as IP
insurance. This means that there will be more data on a scheme’s own claims experience to use
For a scheme new to an insurer there may be very little past claims history available, which
puts the existing insurer at an advantage.
4.3 No claims discounts: No claims discount (NCD) systems are sometimes used for individual and group business
in order to make some allowance for individual claims experience in the premium rates.
Recall that NCD systems are a form of experience rating where an adjustment (usually a discount)
is applied to future premiums. The level of discount depends in some way on the number of
claims made (or on the time expired since last making a claim). This is similar to the NCD scales
that are used for motor insurance premiums in many countries.
Rather than requiring no claims, a ‘low claims discount’ (LCD) approach may be used.
This is similar to NCD, but the premium discount is based on the total amount of claims rather
than the number. This is most common for small groups.
The rationale behind NCD or LCD is that a group or individual that has had good claims experience
in the past is less likely to claim in the future and therefore can be charged lower premiums.
4.4 Different group sizes: Larger groups (more than 50 employees) are often flat rated according to benefit class,
whereas individual business is almost always age rated.
So for smaller groups, some individual rating factors (eg age) will be used in addition to the group
rating factors and the group’s experience.
Large groups frequently self-insure up to a point, eg 125% of expected claims cost, and
purchase administration and stop loss insurance from the insurer.
So the policyholder (ie the employer) meets all claims during the year until (in this example) they
exceed 125% of the expected annual claims cost: after this the insurer meets all the claims. The
insurer also looks after the administration for all claims.
The situation could be summarised, using: Individual business            Individually priced (ie using rating factors such as age,
occupation etc). May have an NCD system.
Small groups                   Premiums based on group rating factors and ages of
individuals; NCD or LCD systems often apply.
Medium groups                  Flat‐rated premiums based on group rating factors, and
Large groups                   Self insurance, usually with stop loss cover and admin from
This table only gives an indication of the size of group most likely to purchase a certain
arrangement. In practice there can be much overlap – for example, a relatively small scheme may
Special characteristics of group busines: Free cover – group schemes may have a free cover limit, below which members will not be
individually underwritten.
Control of the intermediary – An intermediary may influence the data available, so the
insurer, must ensure that the information is accurate and that it is receiving the same level
of information as its competitors. In addition, the insurer would like to exert some influence
over group policyholders in order to reduce work‐related health risks and encourage
claimants back to work, however, this may be hindered by the intermediary.
Limited insured information – Full details of individual members may not be known by the
insurer at the time when the period of insurance begins. In addition, there are likely to be
changes in staff over the period of cover. A deposit premium will be paid up front, followed
by retrospective adjustment premium at the end of the period of cover.
Changes in the workforce – Significant changes to the workforce can alter the riskiness of a
group scheme:
          leavers may be relatively healthy or unhealthy, depending on their reason for leaving
          joiners are likely to healthy (especially if they are young)
          a down‐sizing of the workforce might prompt members to make claims while they
are still covered
          an increase in the size of the workforce might improve claims experience as
newcomers are often younger and fitter, however care should be taken if the
expansion is a result of a merger / acquisition.
Flexible benefits – Flexible benefit schemes increase the risk of anti‐selection by permitting
individuals to have a choice in covers and amounts. The insurer may impose limits and other
restrictions on the choice of such benefits.
\uf0b7 subdivide data into homo:           make adjustments to make appropriate for future experience (both in terms of claim
incidence and claim size – eg due to inflation).
The premiums charged for group policies are dependent on their risk characteristics, which
will be defined in terms of their rating factors. The rating factors used for group business
include individual‐level rating factors (eg age, gender, occupation) and group‐level rating
## RP \uf03d Z \uf0b4 A \uf02b \uf0281 \: where: E is the insurer’s book premium for the group
The credibility factor will depend on the volume (and reliability) of the group experience
data. Premiums calculated for which Z  0 are experience rated.
The burning cost is the estimated cost of claims in the forthcoming insurance period,
calculated from previous years’ experience, adjusted for changes in the numbers insured,
the nature of cover and medical inflation. Yearly burning cost calculations can be used to
compare emerging claim costs over the year against the risk premiums paid. These provide
the earliest possible feedback on premium adequacy.
